name: Free Monad

x.formalRole: Builds a monad "for free" from any functor, representing a program as data (an AST of operations) separate from its interpretation.
x.compositionRule: Free f a is either a pure value or a functor-wrapped continuation; bind appends operations to the tree.
laws: Monad laws hold by construction (the free construction)
x.encoding: An operations functor + a tree type; interpreters (natural transformations) give meaning. Heavyweight outside Haskell/Scala.
x.useCases: DSLs with swappable interpreters (real vs test), separating program description from execution.
misuseRisks: Performance overhead of building/walking the tree
Using it where a simple interface + implementations suffices

def: A construction that turns any functor into a monad, letting you describe a program as an inspectable data structure of operations, then interpret it in multiple ways.
problem: Decouple "what the program does" from "how it is executed", so the same description can be run, tested, or optimised differently.
mechanism: Represents a computation as a tree: leaves are pure values, branches are functor-wrapped continuations. bind grows the tree; an interpreter (natural transformation to a target monad) folds it into real effects.
benefits: Clear separation of description and effect
limits: Often beaten by tagless-final or plain interfaces
failures: Performance cliffs from naive tree interpretation
myths: "Free monads are the way to structure effects" — tagless-final and effect systems are common lighter alternatives
